title: Magic's Guiding Principle
entries: Magic is borrowed, never owned — every spell is a debt the world eventually collects.
To cast is to be noticed; the more you work magic, the more the world learns your name, and not all of it is friendly.
Every spell has a true cost, paid by someone — the trick is making sure it isn't you.
Magic runs on attention: what no one is watching can be changed, what is watched resists.
Power is drawn from memory. Cast too freely and you forget faces, names, and finally your own.
Names are the root of all magic — to know a thing's true name is to hold its leash.
Spells are requests, and the world may decline. It remembers rudeness.
Magic pools in places, not people; a caster is only ever as strong as where they stand.
All magic is inherited grief — it answers best to those with something to mourn.
Casting always leaves a mark: a grey hair, a cold hand, a memory gone.
Nothing is created. To conjure fire here is to steal it from somewhere — and something — else.
Magic is a language, with accents, lies, and words that must never be said aloud.
The dead are the only true experts; every great spell was first taught by a ghost.
Belief is the fuel. A god forgotten is a spell that no longer works.
Magic obeys the logic of stories: the third attempt succeeds, the promise must be kept, the name spoken thrice arrives.
Every caster draws from one shared well of power, and it is running dry.
Blood remembers — magic runs down bloodlines like a debt, or a curse, and skips no one for long.
To work a spell you must give the world something it lacks: a colour, a sound, a year of your life.
Spells are alive and resent being cast; the old ones have opinions.
The moon rules everything. Magic waxes and wanes, and at the dark of it, turns on its user.
Magic is scrupulously honest — it does exactly what you ask, which is rarely what you meant.
Power sleeps in objects, not minds; a peasant with the right ring outmatches any scholar.
Every spell is a small unmaking. Cast enough and you thin, until light passes through you.
The world holds a fixed amount of luck, and magic only moves it from one person to another.
Music is the true grammar of magic; the tone-deaf simply cannot.
Magic answers the desperate and ignores the comfortable — wealth and power dull the gift.
Spells must be witnessed to work; magic performed alone slowly stops being real.
Everything cast returns — the hex, the blessing, the fire — to the hand that threw it, in time.
Magic is a wound in the world, and casters are the ones who keep picking at it.
There are no spells, only bargains, and everything that grants one keeps the receipt.
The gift is a fever: it burns brightest just before it kills you.
Magic flows downhill, from the old and vast to the young and small, and never the other way.
Two casters can never work the same spell the same way; magic is as personal as a signature.
Every working needs an anchor in the real — a bone, a coin, a lock of hair — and the anchor is consumed.
Wards and wishes both fade at dawn; nothing magical survives the honest light of morning unrenewed.
Emotion is the current: rage, love, and terror cast strong and clumsy; calm casts weak and precise.
Magic cannot lie, but it can be made to; the whole art is in the phrasing.
Distance costs — a spell weakens with every mile between caster and target, and the world is very wide.
The first rule is symmetry: to raise one thing you must lower another of equal weight.
Every mage owes a patron they have never met, and the bill always comes due at the worst moment.
Silence is the most dangerous spell; the things that answer it are the oldest.
Magic is contagious — live near a caster long enough and you will start to leak power, want it or not.
You cannot cast the same spell twice in one place; the world scars over and refuses.
The strongest magic is sacrifice, and the world is scrupulous about the exchange rate.
Written spells rot; magic wants to be remembered, not recorded, and punishes those who hoard it in books.
Every caster has one word that will unmake them, and somewhere it is written down.
The dead pay no cost, which is why so much magic ends with someone trying very hard to die correctly.
Power comes from thresholds — dusk, doorways, coastlines, the moment before sleep — and nowhere else.
To master a spell you must first be badly hurt by it; there are no unscarred archmages.
Magic is a loan against tomorrow: every spell borrows from a future that then arrives poorer.
The world grants magic freely to children and takes it back, piece by piece, as they learn to doubt.
Every spell needs a true believer in the room, and it does not have to be the caster.
